Great work!  The YAML anchor method works well for me!  Much thanks!

I propose an new (and already working) design pattern for leveraging anchors: A top-level section called "anchors".  Looks cleaner without random blocks hanging about.

Also worth noting that I could poll on the get where thing should show up, which means I wouldn't have to look at the job endpoint.  For this present API though I'll stick with this for now. 

```
defaults:
  request_headers:
    Accept: application/json

anchors:

    track_job: &track_job
        GET: /job/$RESPONSE['$.JobSubmit.id']
        status: 200
        poll:
          count: 4
          delay: 15
        response_json_paths:
          $.job.percentageCompleted: 100
          $.job.status: "Closed"

tests:

  - name: create thing blue
    POST: /thing
    status: 202
    data:
      color: blue
      description: This is a description.

  - name: track create thing blue
    <<: *track_job

  - name: create thing red
    POST: /thing
    status: 202
    data:
      color: red
      description: This is a description.

  - name: track create thing
    <<: *track_job

```

I think that queues should be the preferred mechanism for communicating with curio tasks from other contexts.    If threads are being used, use a thread Queue object and the curio `abide()` method as described here:  http://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#synchronizing-with-threads-and-processes

Initially, I though that a queue could also be used to communicate between synchronous space and asynchronous space.  Now, I'm not so sure.  Yes, it can be done, but maybe it's enough to just use a deque or something simpler.   Coroutines are weird--you can create them externally and pass them into curio where they can be awaited later.  I need to think about that a bit more. 

I wasn't planning to give the low-level curio kernel itself any kind of API that allows it to be controlled from outside threads or non-async code.   Isolation is part of the whole design.  You interact with the kernel via async/await.  Always.

Tried something different. The idea is that everyone shares the time determined by `time.value`, which is continuously polled by `worker_timer`. I think it gives everyone the same time, but it does take up an extra process...

```python
import ctypes
import multiprocessing as mp
from psychopy import core

def worker(st, time):
    timer = core.Clock()
    timer.reset()
    timer.add(0.1)
    for ii in range(100):
        print(st + str(time.value))
        while timer.getTime() < 0:
            pass
        timer.add(0.1)

def worker_timer(time, pp):
    while pp.value:
        time.value = core.getTime()

if __name__=='__main__':
    time = mp.Value('d', 0)
    poisonpill = mp.Value(ctypes.c_bool)
    poisonpill.value = True
    proc_remote = mp.Process(target=worker, args=('worker: ', time))
    proc_timer = mp.Process(target=worker_timer, args=(time, poisonpill))
    
    proc_timer.start()
    core.wait(0.5) # give time for timer process to start
    proc_remote.start() # start remote process
    core.wait(0.1)
    worker('main: ', time) # start main process
    proc_remote.join()
    poisonpill.value = False # after main is done, end timer
```

> As it's name suggests, reraise is for reraising an existing exception, possibly with a different traceback. 

In my situation, I want to raise an existing exception with a different exception type and the same traceback. 

In Python 2, I do like this::
```
try:
     raise XXXException()
except Exception:
     t, v, tb = sys.exc_info()
     raise CustomException, v, tb
```

In Python 3, I can implement like this::
```
try:
     raise XXXException()
except Exception:
     t, v, tb = sys.exc_info()
     raise CustomException(v).with_traceback(tb)
```
I expect my codebase to be 2&3 compatible, I choose `six.reraise` to do such thing.


so, I modify the code to::
```
try:
     raise XXXException()
except Exception:
     t, v, tb = sys.exc_info()
     six.reraise(CustomException, v, tb)
```
> I'm not sure what you expect to happen on Python2, since it has no concept of exception chaining.

But the problem is that, as I stated above, `six.reraise` not working as expected (for me) in Python 3, but it works in Python 2. It raises a XXXException(original) in Python 3, and it raise a CustomException in Python 2. 

What I supposed `six.reraise` to raise is a `CustomException` instead of `XXXException` in both Python 2 and 3.

Note that in YANG 1, a submodule cannot "see" any definitions in the main module.  It can only see definitions in included submodules.  The common solution to this is to let the main module only contain include statements, and move all data definition statements to a submodule  called (in this case) "sub-common", and let "sub-main" include "sub-common".

This has been changed in YANG 1.1, but pyang does not yet handle this case.

@gengshenghong If you are willing to install and use Python 3.5, I would suggest using [pymata-aio](https://github.com/MrYsLab/pymata-aio), that is much more CPU efficient than PyMata.

If that is not acceptable, I will make the following comments.

1. If you are running a multi-core processor (I think you are, since you mention that you saw CPU utilization of 110%. The percentage is total across all cores.), you should be able to run other applications simultaneously without issue. The high CPU utilization should not limit the functionality of your PyMata application.

2.  PyMata needs to continuously monitor the serial port for data changes. This is to allow for immediate updates to its database and to call any registered callbacks. Pyserial does not provide an event driven interface, and so it must be polled. Hence the high CPU utilization.

3. Python cannot run multiple threads across multiple cores.  This is a limitation due to the Python GIL.  PyMata uses a threading concurrency model.

pymata-aio on the other hand, does not use a threading concurrency model, but uses asyncio and does not suffer from this limitation.

If you have any other questions or comments, please let me know.

it's been a while since I investigated this issue, but it still seems to be reproducible easily. However the file URL was not pointing directly to the actual file, this should work better:

I didn't want to track state of the configuration change and I didn't want to automatically change the configuration.

So I added an argument you can pass that will allow this to happen.

```
self.allow_auto_change
```

The argument will default to false, but you can override it.

Netmiko won't track state of these terminal settings, however. 

So it will be the responsibility of the Netmiko user to restore state, if they need to (i.e. if you use this argument on the ASA and then perform a `wr mem`, you would need to set the terminal width to what you want it to be).

> So for us, it doesn't matter what io provides, we just need a way to easily use or copy out the evaluation function itself and feed it with the data in the form of some standard Python data structure such as a list of tuples.

Cool, thanks for the feedback!

The current api uses a list of interval arrays.  I think this should be simple enough to construct from what you describe: each entry in the list is a flat segmentation, and the segmentations are ordered by level.  This is intended to be as close to the flat segmentation metrics' interface as possible.



> You could provide some example for parsing these files,

The trouble here is that it would make the interface to the eval script pretty nasty.  Instead of the usual
```bash
./task_eval.py ref_file est_file arguments
```
we'd need something like
```bash
./task_eval.py -r ref_file_0 -r ref_file_1 -r ref_file_2 -e est_file_0 -e est_file_1 -e est_file_2  arguments
```
and I don't think anyone wants that.

An alternative would be an intermediate parser/converter script that smashes multiple csv files into a sane format (eg, the 4-tuple of ``start, end, label, level`` described above), and then we can go back to the simpler eval script format.  I prefer this approach, but it's still hackish IMO.

There are too many (sometimes even conflicting) conventions. For sure the configargparse library should not be too restrictive and let the application developers choose the most fitting convention for their use case.

Actually, the `action="store"` for config arguments is [already enforced](https://github.com/bw2/ConfigArgParse/blob/master/configargparse.py#L744), but it does not check the `default=` parameter. I guess the argparse devs did not forbid combining `action="store"` and `default=[...]` for a reason, after all it is not the end users that use the library. I think that releasing the requirement on `action="store"` for config arguments to accept also `action="append"` would be more fitting for the library.

Rather than introducing null values, which may depend on the config file format, perhaps we should add another parameter for the `ArgumentParser` class to specify how many configs should be read at most? I can think of many programs for both sides of the argument (e.g. [makepkg](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Makepkg) sources `~/.config/pacman/makepkg.conf` on top of `/etc/makepkg.conf`, but in e.g. [dhcpcd](http://roy.marples.name/projects/dhcpcd/index) or [dnsmasq](http://www.thekelleys.org.uk/dnsmasq/doc.html) the user configuration overrides the default) and it would be a shame if configargparse is too restrictive.

Looks like I've found the cause of this problem. When a package is installed from a 'wheel', 'entry_points' are processed by 'pip' and resulting scripts doesn't contain `# EASY-INSTALL-...` string. If a package is installed with `pip install --no-use-wheel` or `python setup.py install` command, command line scripts are being created by setuptools and contain such lines, so the global completion works as expected.

Sorry about the lengthy delay in my response to this. I unfortunately don't know enough about configargparse to figure out why this happens. Ultimately I'm surprised argcomplete works with configargparse to any extent at all. Beacuse argparse is not actually built to allow customization of the argument parsing process, argcomplete relies on runtime monkey-patching of the parser (conditional upon detecting that argument completion has been invoked). Of course this monkey-patching has no API contract to stand on, so it's brittle and will break if you replace the argparse parser with something else.

I'll try to read more into what configparse does to see if argcomplete can be more defensive. If you want to give it a try, see the "Debugging" section in the readme.

I have an attempt at a solution ready [here](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/tree/fixup-listeners).

It fixes the issue by postponing handling and sending the keyboard event until all system hooks have executed by posting a message to the listener main loop. According to the documentation of [LowLevelKeyboardProc](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms644985(v=vs.85).aspx), the keyboard state is not updated during the system hook callback, and thus `GetKeyboardState` will return an incorrect shift state.

Please note that this bug fix required a rather large rewrite of the *Windows* backend. I have not yet merged the fixup branch into master as I will need to add some tests, and figure out a way to actually have the tests pass on Windows.

I would appreciate your feedback on the bugfix, especially if actually resolves the issues of your use case, and whether you notice any regressions.

I took a time to think about and...you may be right: I cannot figure any case when you would need to have whole unicode group + some chars (say `Nd` (numbers) and only ascii letters). My initial intentions to provide "exact" argument based on semantic reasons. `characters(ascii_letters)` looks more friendly than`sampled_from(ascii_letters)`.

So, let's limit ourself with unicode groups for now. It would be easy to return this feature if someone find a need in it.

As for second argument, I like your idea with having just `categories`. Having both whitelist and blacklist is a bit strange: while it's possible to specify both, there is no case when it'll be valid.

So, summary:
1. Change `characters` signature to `categories` which accepts either container or callable;
2. For container argument characters emits only those chars which are belongs to the specified unicode categories;
3. If callable is provided, it must return true to produce the character or false to not;
4. By default, all chars are produces except surrogates;

Which leads to:
```
characters(lambda _: True) -> all categories
characters() -> all categories except Cs
characters({'Nd', 'Nl', 'No'}) -> all numbers
characters(lambda category: category not in {'Nd', 'Nl', 'No'}) -> all not numbers
```

While I don't like a bit blacklisting here, but I found this case quite rare one to really get annoyed.

At either rate, this check for is_active_input is probably not necessary
since, if CEC is properly enabled and the active app is not Background, the
Chromecast should switch the input over automatically anyway. Another
option would be to create a global list in the pychromecast module called
something like DISABLE_CEC that can be populated with friendly names of
devices for which CEC related values should be ignored. It could even use
the fnmatch module <https://docs.python.org/3/library/fnmatch.html> to
allow for wildcards in friendly names. This feature could then be forwarded
to the Home Assistant config file. Just a thought.

I've also thought about this, e.g. by defining some `IFS` (internal field separator), like with bash's `read` command. Then we can have an optimized function for reading whitespace (or any separator defined by IFS).

But then you need to worry about the cases where you DO want the whitespace, e.g., this contrived example for matching variable identifiers:

```
variable = varstart varcontinue*
varstart = ~"[a-z]"
varcontinue = ~"[a-z0-9]"
```

Here you would want to match `x1` as a variable name, but not `x 1`.

To get around this, you could use different rule operators, e.g. use `=` for regular rules and `:=` for rules that ignore IFS strings. Or you could have a special group inside a rule (`variable = (: varstart varcontinue* :)`), where `(: ... :)` is the grouping that doesn't allow IFS in between the terms.

Hey, the JWT is base64 encoded, so you'd need to use a base64 decode to get the information stored in the first two parts of the JWT (the 3rd part is the secure sign).

So, for your example (https://www.base64decode.org/):
The first part of the JWT gives ('part' being the data up to the first period)
{"alg":"HS256","exp":1432115032,"iat":1432115002}

And the second part gives (between first period and second period)
{"user_id":1}

Here is a good discussion of JWT structure: https://developer.atlassian.com/static/connect/docs/latest/concepts/understanding-jwt.html

So, your JWT helper needs to do a base64 decode, and then parse JSON structures to give you what you want. 

Please note that due to using itsdangerous under the hood, there are a few quirks where data is stored, see: https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-jwt/issues/40

Also, to make your life easier, consider using Satellizer (https://github.com/sahat/satellizer) - handles a lot of the grunt stuff behind the scenes.

Python threads aren't true/full multithreading. There's some talk about it [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12758952/green-threads-and-thread-in-python). It's threading model works well for when you are trying to parallelize multiple http requests at once and waiting on the servers to do some work (in parallel) before dealing with the responses. As you've seen though it doesn't actually allow work to happen in parallel on the client side so it's not helpful for trying to parallelize uploads. You'll need to do multiple processes to get that, not really a case that requests-futures was designed for.

    self._socket = ssl.wrap_socket(socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM))

to this:

    self._socket = ssl.wrap_socket(socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM), ssl_version=ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv3)

This forces the use of SSLv3.

Oh, If you mean to migrate content of multi_key_dict and make db work as if it were multi_key_dict? I'm not sure. Mind that multi_key_dict, once populated, should allow you to retrieve items with expected constant (O(1)) time (as a standard dict() which multi_key_dict is build on). Now if you wanted to use the DB to store / restore the multi_key_dict (e.g. as a persistent storage), but later wanted to use multi_key_dict object - it will give you that speed (just remember to back-it-up on exit etc). If, however, you wished to keep the interface of multi_key_dict and have the underlying storage in the DB, that depends on how you implement it: possibly using composite index?. I guess if the later is your goal (and not just a persistent storage of multi_key_dict) implementing it in the DB should work better than re-creating multi_key_dict (O(n)) fetched from DB every time you need to use multi_key_dict (especially if you only then need to search for one (or few) items),  although it might still make sense - all depends on your use-case.     from threading import Thread
    ui_thread = Thread(target=screen_loop, args=(screen,))
    ui_thread.start()

    screen.clear()
    # ... setup screen ...

I think the _right_ solution to apply to this issue now, is to simply raise a meaningful exception when an in clause has more than 2000 items. This is by far the easiest solution for now, until something further is done to mitigate this, and then it is trivial to split up ones own code.

I still find it feasible to split up the request on the `IN` parameters, but this is hard, because other split-ups must be taken into consideration.
Alternatively the parameters can be typed out for each chunk, as I did in my modified code above. this will make it much easier to handle other clauses, but will make it impossible to use `executemany`, and we will lose the potential optimizations from this one.

A third solution might be to split up the `IN` clause in the same SQL statement, as well as typing it out. I will try this out before I need to move on.

Just in case anybody cares, I think I figured the licensing situation out.

TL;DR: This project does not import any GPLed code, so this issue is moot.

There are two different major forks of python-gnupg:

1. The original fork is [Vinay Sajip's fork](https://bitbucket.org/vinay.sajip/python-gnupg) (old [Google Code repo](https://code.google.com/archive/p/python-gnupg/), [mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/python-gnupg)). This is also the fork that is registered as [python-gnupg on PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/python-gnupg). This fork is [3-clause BSD licensed](https://bitbucket.org/vinay.sajip/python-gnupg/src/afbe4e74cfc7fb79dde344499e8934c88874ef85/LICENSE.txt?fileviewer=file-view-default) since at least version 0.2.7 ([Google Code Issue on license clarification](https://code.google.com/archive/p/python-gnupg/issues/24)).
2. The second fork is [Isis Agora Lovecruft's fork](https://github.com/isislovecruft/python-gnupg), "including security patches, extensive documentation, and extra features". When Isis forked from Sajip in version 0.3.2, for some reason the [license](https://github.com/isislovecruft/python-gnupg/commit/69b7f865299db2cda71b9ba0f4319eefec908f9d#diff-9879d6db96fd29134fc802214163b95a) was AGPLv3. The license was later changed to GPLv3 in [commit 6b154c294e373f167de600378e1abd00ad8b0a83](https://github.com/isislovecruft/python-gnupg/commit/6b154c294e373f167de600378e1abd00ad8b0a83#diff-9879d6db96fd29134fc802214163b95a) ([full commit history of the LICENSE file](https://github.com/isislovecruft/python-gnupg/commits/master/LICENSE)).

I've dug through the 0.2.6 and 0.3.2 tarballs from the [Google Code downloads page](https://code.google.com/archive/p/python-gnupg/downloads), and both are licensed under the 3-clause BSD.

Luckily, django-email-extras depends on the version in PyPI (Sajip's version), which is 3-clause BSD licensed, so we can entirely ignore the interplay between Python modules and the GPL.

This is, unfortunately, a conceptual error. Check out this introduction to [descriptors](https://www.ibm.com/developerworks/library/os-pythondescriptors/index.html), which my give you some more detailed insight into why having a reference to objects on a descriptor doesn't work out.

In short though, consider three facts:

1. Traits are class attributes (that is, they are owned by the class).
2. One class can have many instances (or objects).
3. All instances reference the same class attributes (that is, they don't own them).

All this implies that a single trait is associated with **all** instances of a class, and thus is "owned" by no single instance. Thus, the idea of associating a trait with any one object that may reference it, is a bit contradictory.

This change is meant to provide the hooks so that I can do all the extensions in widgets, with minimal changes in the traitlets. 
 - the addition of the base class really cannot have an impact functionally. 
 - the change on instance_init makes it closer to what it was before, and more consistent with the documentation.

+1 on option A1, as it's more pythonic.  Python does not generally chain methods onto constructors, and usually methods that mutate state (like `tag` does in this case) would return None, not the object.  The thing I don't like about A1 is that it is the most verbose of all the options, which is almost enough for me to vote for B3.
The separation of `BaseDescriptor` and `HasDescriptors` should allow anyone to experiment with descriptors having a different logic than the core traitlets if really needed.

@erikbern, that's a really good concern actually. I'm not sure either if people care/use namespaces. But here's some related facts and thoughts:

 * At Spotify I thought it was hard to know where tasks are defined when there's no namespace. Compare the task family `IngestUsersToDbTask` vs `discover_weekly.ingestions.IngestUsersToDbTask`. Also the problem when two different teams have a task named `IngestUsersToDbTask`, the luigi server cannot distinguish the task families then.
 * I've recently improved documentation about what namespaces is.
 * In an follow up PR I'll try to make it so that instead of `luigi --module hello.world hello.world.MyTask --my-param 123` you'll just write `luigi hello.world.MyTask --my-param 123`. The increased readability and enforced consistency I find to be great wins. Also I tend to like frameworks with conventions so that I don't need to think for myself.
 * There have also been a few others sending PRs about namespaces, so I think some people care.

I spent a lot of time on this and i hope it benefits someone else that has also looked into this time-suck, near-fruitless endeavor:

    elbs = client.describe_load_balancers()

    for elb in elbs:
        #Get Named Policy to pass to get the active policy. -1 denotes the last in the list.
        policy_name = jmespath.search('ListenerDescriptions[].PolicyNames[] | [-1]', elb)

        policy_description = client.describe_load_balancer_policies(LoadBalancerName=elb, PolicyNames=[policyname])
        console_policy = jmespath.search('PolicyDescriptions[?PolicyName==`{0}`] | [0].PolicyAttributeDescriptions[0].AttributeValue'.format(policyname), policy_description)

        return console_policy

After thinking about it, I'm not even sure if this is something we'd want to support.  What happens in python3 if you don't open the file in binary would be:

```
<some encoded file> ---> python3 will decode to str() --> http.client sends data using iso-8859-1.
```

This is almost never what you would want and has potential to break.  We could also just say that if we detect you've given us a text file, we'll encode it to utf-8 before sending it over to the http client, but it seems to make more sense to just require the user to be explicit and give us a binary file.  That way they have complete control.

It is, indeed. The root issue is that Pipenv’s resolver (which is basically pip-tools) lacks a feature called backtracking. Think of it like save-load points in an RPG video game—you save before you make a decision, and then play on to see what happens. If you don’t like the outcome, load and do things differently, until you get what you want. If nothing you do is satisfying, load an earlier save and try again from there, and so on.

Backtracking is not a simple thing to implement. It is not difficult (basically an already-solved problem), but requires significant resources to implement, which the project lacks. Please do try to work on this issue if you’re interested, we will be very, very thankful for your effort.

Patch generation is related inasmuch as it's a *prerequisite* to effectively getting changes to stick, but I'm not seeing how it itself results in pipenv being able to function with devendored dependencies. I'd see it as a "dependent issue needing to be resolved before this one can be worked on", not "part of this issue". All it really says is "great, now we can finally *get started* on trying to make devendoring work".

I'm not seeing how licensing is related at all. It's a completely separate issue that simply happened to be discovered during the course of someone's attempts to devendor pipenv. In fact, it's only when pip is vendored that there's licensing issues at all -- removing them (by devendoring them) ***automatically*** resolves the licensing issues, because the only thing that needs to be licensed is pipenv itself, as the devendored dependencies coming from the system already come with licenses.

@techalchemy I'd love to keep this discussion focused on the bug report. I don't know whether there is a history with previous bug reports that were filed in a non-constructive way, and if so, I really understand your pain. It is clear that everyone on this project is a volunteer, and you are perfectly right, people coming to this issue tracker shouldn't just complain about things being broken.

At the same time, there are various ways to commit to a project. Making a pull request with a working solution to a problem is definitely the strongest way to commit, but the barrier of entry for outsiders for a significantly complicated project such as pipenv is very high. Another way to contribute is to provide a simple test case to narrow down the problem, which the author of this bug, @jkp, has done.

You may find his `test.sh` script unnecessary; I personally very much enjoyed it - I used his example code to verify that the bug can be reproduced on my machine as well, and then commented in this thread to confirm that I can reproduce it. I have seen enough cases where a reported bug could not be reproduced on a different machine because of one person's weird local machine configuration that I've come to value independent verifications of a bug a lot.

If all of this sounds reasonable to you, I'd be very grateful if you reopened the ticket. Please don't hesitate to ask for further information or other test cases if it helps in narrowing down the issue - I'm hopeful that this way, we can split the workload in debugging this, and ultimately help on improving what is already a fantastic package manager for Python.

> so I don't risk to install packages outside the venv

@andreagrandi the idea of pipenv is that rather than using pip to install things, you call `pipenv install`.  This way you don't run the risk of accidentally installing things outside of your project virtualenv. 

>I still prefer to have the name always visible in my command prompt

Since it's based on where your `Pipfile` is, all that matters is which directory you're in, which is always visible from the command prompt, you'll already be able to tell which project `pipenv install` works on.

> Also: very often I try things using ipython in the shell before writing the proper code in my IDE

I do this too, it's very handy.  I include `ptpython` in all of my pipfiles in the `dev-packages` section.  Instead of having to know the location of the virtualenv and make sure I have activated the correct one, I now offload all of this to pipenv by simply calling `pipenv run ptpython`,  Honestly, it seems like pipenv would solve a lot of problems for you if you adopted this workflow.  If you are going to change toolsets but make no modification to your usage, it's probably going to be somewhat difficult.

> Last but not least, if you use Visual Studio Code (but it's true even for Sublime and probably Atom too) and you want to make sure the code completion works properly, you have to start ./code from the terminal

I use vscode as my main editor and I have never started it from the terminal.  All I've done is set in my user settings file:

```json
{
    "python.venvPath": "~/.local/share/virtualenvs", // or whatever your $WORKON_HOME setting is
}
```

And then in my workspace for each project:
```
{
    "python.pythonPath": "~/.local/share/virtualenvs/pipenv-MfOPs1lW/bin/python",
    "python.linting.flake8Enabled": true,
    "python.linting.pylintEnabled": false
}
```

You can for example write a script to generate a file for each project in your `.vscode` folder which takes the output of `pipenv --venv` after you've run `pipenv install` and puts the path to the interpreter in your settings file. Alternatively, you can `ctrl+shift+p` which works in vscode and sublime, not sure about atom, and type 'interpreter' and set your interpreter that way as long as you've done the first bit of telling the editor where to look.

So I understand why you need the results you need, but in most cases I think pipenv can actually make things easier for you if you'll modify the way you use it just a touch. We have a lot of areas for improvement, but to get the full advantage of switching to pipenv also requires a bit of adjustment

@agronholm I apologize. I didn't mean to be rejecting #1140 outright. It did highlight two alternate paths, vendoring OrderedDict or dropping support for Python 2.6, such that the `namespace_packages` declaration can be re-used. But now that I think about it more, we probably don't want to re-use that declaration, because it does more than simply declare namespace packages. It also signals the behavior that creates `-nspkg.pth` files and other namespace package management, which we may not want conflated with package discovery.

As I re-read this issue and consider the two PRs, I guess there is a consensus that we should add this interface. We should probably base the solution on #943 as I promised to do there.

> the only way to do this is via setup.cfg or settings `packages` to `find:`

I'm not sure I follow. And the declarative config, including `find:` didn't exist at the time. Does `find:` already support declaration of namespace packages?

That said, I do think we should keep the explicit kwarg - so when these functions get generalized to color or 3D/N-D images, the contributor will come across this kwarg and be prompted to check into how `gaussian` operates differently.

The problem really is that our `gaussian`, as currently implemented, has magic if we aren't explicit. In the default case with  `multichannel=None`, it tries to determine if a color image is passed - if the input is 3D and has a final dimension of 3, it assumes it's color. Explicitly specifying this right now makes future contributors' jobs easier.

> Three specific concerns I have:
> 
> - we should aim to be compatible with https://github.com/numpy/numpy-stubs, even though they are just experimental for now.

Agreed.

> - I'm wondering whether we can add some kind of semantic information to our types. For example, an Array2D can be a grayscale image, or it can be a list of coordinates. Should we make two separate typedefs for this?

Short answer – yes – for why see below.

> - Similarly, an Array3D can be a multichannel 2D image or it can be a 3D grayscale image. Also, some algorithms take an nD image: do we add an ArrayND type that encompasses any dimension/shape?

Yes. Using [`NewType`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#newtype)s we can easily create subtypes of our derived types.

Here's an example type tree:
```
                             –––– CoordinatesND
            –––– Array2D –––|
           |                 –––– Greyscale2D
ArrayND –––|
           |                 –––– Multichannel2D
            –––– Array3D –––|
                             –––– Greyscale3D
```
You could additionally add a `GreyscaleND` type under `ArrayND`, `Coordinates2D` under `CoordinatesND`, etc.

> regarding automatic annotations, I propose the following:
> 
> - the automatic annotator should ignore signatures that already have type annotations

Definitely.

> - the automatic annotator should fail when it doesn't understand a signature, and point users to the signature so that it can be manually annotated.

Agreed; I have the framework set up for this already! Another thing to note: do we want to automatically abstract up one level derived numerical types (i.e. `uint8 -> UnsignedInteger`, `float64 -> Float`, etc.)?

Another clear example of how ceph-deploy an make a huge leap in user friendliness by moving to MVC:

All commands can have a flag:

    --overwrite-conf

Without this flag many operations should fail, if the config remotely does not match the local config, and in a user friendly way should fail before any other operations have been attempted.

With MVC, you have a model, that gathered the desired state, a way to establish the current state, a way to compare states. So before any operation is started you can check if you do need the flag.

Without MVC, you are likely to only be able to unify the code to check the need for the flag to allow configuration over writing at some point when you attempt to overwrite the config, which may or may not be the most user friendly / or code friendly place in the config setup.

```
    segment.updateSynapses(synToDec, - _permDec, _permMax, _permConnected, removed);

The above line can cause remove of some elements from vector ```_synapses```. In this case the index numbers in```inactiveSegmentIndices``` may become invalid. 

For example ```_synapses``` may hold 10 elements and ```inactiveSegmentIndices``` may  hold values ```[0,1,2,7,8]```. In this case  I believe that the correct values of```inactiveSegmentIndices``` are ```[4,5]``` but since it was not updated after line 1656 of Cells4.cpp it still contains same values ```[0,1,2,7,8]``` which are invalid.

In my code, I just write a init log function. This function will be called first in the new process.

```python
def init_log(sentry_dsn, sentry_level=logging.ERROR):
    import raven
    from raven.handlers.logging import SentryHandler
    from raven.transport.registry import TransportRegistry, default_transports
    raven.Raven = None
    raven.Client.logger = logging.getLogger('raven')
    raven.Client._registry = TransportRegistry(transports=default_transports)
    client = raven.Client(sentry_dsn)
    handler = SentryHandler(client)
    handler.setLevel(sentry_level)
    logger.addHandler(handler)
```

Or you can simply don't use log before fork process.

geo = Namespace('http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#')

print(list(g.namespace_manager.namespaces()))
g.namespace_manager.bind('n80', not_geo, override=True, replace=True)
g.namespace_manager.bind('geo', geo, override=True, replace=True)
print(list(g.namespace_manager.namespaces()))

For sync slaves, as per postgresql documentation:
```
synchronous_standby_names - The first named standby will be used as the synchronous standby. Standbys listed after this will take over the role of synchronous standby if the first one should fail.
```
What one has to do is to list all servers in the desired order. I am not sure how postgres will react if the first server in the list is the actual master though. But if it works (have to test it) it will overcome the fast switching of sync slave (unless some node fails and comes back online in a short amount of time).

As per my understanding according to: http://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/runtime-config-wal.html
If one sets synchronous_commit to on or remote_write, the master will reply to commits only after the slave receives the data as well (even if it was not written to disk yet, it is visible in postgres). Sorry to chime in, I have three things to say about the inverted/normal droput thing : 

- It doesn't make a difference for simple feed forward nets, see these results obtained with my mnist example from #217 : 

![mnist_drop_vs_inv_drop](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10391785/21204416/5411b920-c225-11e6-94c7-c4204528f339.png)

(Accuracy on the test set over 50 epochs for a 3-layers feed forward net with dropout on the first to layers (p=0.2))

- I discussed it with @neubig but maybe the problem with inverted dropout in RNNs is that the 1/(1-p) factor accumulates in the gradient, so over t timesteps the gradient is 1/(1-p)^t times the gradient for standard dropout.

- Also I double checked and for reference Keras and Tensorflow use inverted dropout as their standard dropout so it shouldn't make that big of a diffenrence

Here's my expected behaviour for the following scenarios:

> flaky internet
Retry up to N times, otherwise fail that download; continue the other jobs. Bubble up to the user that certain downloads have failed.

> hard reset
"Resume" job. Support for resuming would depend on the type of job (downloads and file transfers can be resumeable, but anything else is ??).

> Corrupt DB
Have a check during startup to see if the DB is readable (it should almost always be, it's SQLite). If it can't read it, clear it out.

So this is applicable in situations where docker is running alongside docker (e.g  girder worker running in a container and a task running in a container).  Currently the TemporaryNamedVolume container tries to mount  the worker container's tmp directory into the task container's tmp directory.  Because the worker is talking to  the docker engine via a mounted socket file,  it is **actually** talking to the docker engine running on the host machine.  That means when the worker container makes the request to mount ```/tmp/whatever/foo.jpg``` inside the task container,  it is passed to the docker engine running on the host,  and the docker engine ***mounts the host's /tmp/whatever/foo.jpg directory*** inside the task container.  This requires that the host /tmp directory be mounted in the worker container's /tmp directory so that the host, the worker and the task all share the same /tmp directory. 

mounting the host /tmp directory into the worker /tmp directory must be performed *at deploy time* (for instance [here](https://github.com/girder/girder_worker/blob/master/tests/integration/docker-compose.yml#L33-L34).

Alternately,  if we used dockerpy  to create a docker manged volume,  that volume would exist on the host at /var/run/docker/....  and could be mounted *at run time*  inside the worker container  and the task container.

Without the pinned version of dependencies, you don't have a fully reproducible environment.  This means even with celery pinned to 3.1.23, dependencies of celery will be different depending on how and when it was installed.  Requirements files are intended to describe a specific deployment or testing environment, not a list a dependencies.

The trouble with requirements files as we are using them is that they only work for producing a reproducible environment if all dependencies are given in a single file.  When you install from multiple requirements files into a virtual environment (i.e for plugins), you lose the reproducibility they are intended to provide.  In this case, I would argue that we should drop all pretense of trying create a reproducible environment and just use the relaxed dependencies given in the setup.py.

We are making huge improvements in our billing stack. The API is not yet stable, and will change in the future. We should probably document it somewhere.

```python
import datetime

from scaleway.apis.api_billing import BillingAPI

ORGANIZATION_ID = 'xxx'
AUTH_TOKEN = 'xxx'
INVOICE_ID = 'xxx'

INVOICE_YEAR = 2017
INVOICE_MONTH = 1

api = BillingAPI(auth_token=AUTH_TOKEN)

invoice_date = datetime.datetime(
    year=INVOICE_YEAR, month=INVOICE_MONTH, day=1
).isoformat()


# Mandatory hack: x-auth-token must be given with **kwargs since it currently *must* contain dashes and not underscores, and be in the URL
kwargs = {
    'format': 'pdf',
    'x-auth-token': AUTH_TOKEN
}
ret = api.query().invoices(ORGANIZATION_ID)(invoice_date + '+00:00')(INVOICE_ID).get(**kwargs)

# ret contains the PDF, you can save it with open('invoice.pdf', 'w+').write(ret)
print ret
```

I will add this in. I've talked to our format expert. For an integer, the two formats will do the same thing. Only for decimal values will it behave different. So, either is fine here. The one caveat though, is that the integer value will only be correct up to about 16 digits anyway. As these values are floats internal to SAS, when they get larger than 16 digits, they will not necessarily be accurate when converted to integers.
Since this is specifically a row count for the table, 16 digits gets you into the quadrillions range, which, is still pretty big for a single table, even these days. So I think this should be a good fix for some time :)

def method_involving_motifs(x, params):

There are now two incompatible proposals (the latter a counter-proposal by Parity Tech due to their implementation specifics):

* [EIP-1087: Net gas metering for SSTORE operations](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-1087)
* [EIP-1283: Net gas metering for SSTORE without dirty maps](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-1283)

-----

A copy-paste from [gitter/ethereum/AllCoreDevs](https://gitter.im/ethereum/AllCoreDevs?at=5b6f2bf4a6af14730b2088a4):

> @veox
> 
> (...)
> 
> **EIP-1087 introduces a map (of `bool`s) for marking "dirty" storage,** but doesn't make comparisons to the original values until the post-processing step. Post-processing can be delayed until a transaction's execution is complete, so that the refund logic can be applied outside the EVM.
> 
> EIP-1283 needs a "cache" (a map, most likely - of `uint256`s) of original storage values, so that it can determine gas_used when doing a write (20000 or 5000). This cache must now necessarily reside within the EVM. In other words:
> 
> **EIP-1283 introduces a map (of `uint256`s) for "original" value look-ups.**
> 
> (The above might not make sense depending on how your favourite implementation delineates "inside the EVM". Or maybe I'm battling a chimera here, to understand the trade-offs. Sorry if noise.)

To which the author of EIP-1283 responds:

> @sorpaas
> 
> Not quite the case, actually. EIP-1283's "map" is needed to be kept for every client right now -- all clients already have this information because they need to handle it in case a transaction reverts. Just like a storage slot's _current value_ and _new value_, most clients won't need any new extra structs to fetch that info.
> 
> The issue with EIP-1087 is that the "dirty map" concept is in conflict with one of the optimization we do after EIP-658, so for parity-ethereum, we can't have that without getting an extra struct.
> 
> So you may say:
> 
> * EIP-1087 needs a map (of `uint256`s) for "original" value look-ups, and introduces a map (of `bool`s) for marking "dirty" storage.
> * EIP-1283 needs a map (of `uint256`s) for "original" value look-ups.
> 
> The first is needed any way by other operations as well.

-----

A few questions, somewhat abstract:

* Which of the two (if either) would more closely describe the situation in `py-evm`?
* Would implementing either require pushing the "cache for original values of storage items" deeper? Or is that already available at the point where gas-use metering is done?

Another thing:

It's typically better to settle on something called a threat model before you start implementing protections against particular attacks. The reason for this is just so you have some idea of what you're trying to protect against, because there is no such thing as a completely secure system, just ones that protect against particular attacks. Knowing what you're trying to protect against will influence what choices you want to make.

An example using pip (since it's what I'm most familar):

We currently operate under a threat model which says that the operators of the repository (e.g. PyPI), the machines running the repository, as well as the authors of the projects that the user has chosen to install are considered "trusted" and we don't attempt to protect against any of them being malicious. This means that our threat model are network attackers who sit between the user executing pip and PyPI, but does not include a malicious repository administrator, or a compromised machine running PyPI.

Looking at that threat model, TLS is the best fit for us right now since it is designed to protect against network attackers but not anyone on either side of the connection. 

In the future we intend to expand that threat model to include other attackers and types of attacks, but by defining the threat model first, we give ourselves a set of success criteria to evaluate any potential solution against and we guard against scope creep where we try to protect against extra stuff by making things more complicated without fully thinking through to figure out what threat we're trying to protect against and whether or not the total solution actually protects against it or whether the system is vulnerable to the software equivalent of:

As an update, I noticed that I can mitigate this issue from the CLI by running the following command: `az appservice web config update --node-version 6.6.0`. I suppose this is somewhat analogous to needing to run `az appservice web config container update` to set a custom container. However, the problem is that you can setup an app and push a Git deployment without realizing the app wasn't configured correctly. Whereas with the custom Docker container, you wouldn't expect it to know which image you want it to use.

Ideally, the web app would be able to auto-detect your runtime stack by inspecting the code upon deployment (doesn't Kudu do this already?).

The Python code still stores source data, it's the same code you wrote :-) The file is `cache.json` for ESRI sources and is in GeoJSON format. I'm proposing changing that to `cache.csv`.

I don't think that CSV format is much further from the ESRI JSON source than GeoJSON is, they're both transformations of the data. Smashing the geometry to a centroid does degrade the source data though.

If being "close to the original format" is a goal maybe that `cache.json` file should be ESRI JSON and we transform it later, writing a ESRI `ijson` parser for the conform step? That's about as much work as writing a GeoJSON `ijson` parser.

Note that the only GeoJSON sources we see now are ESRI; there are no other types of GeoJSON sources in our collection. There might be some day, but beware that writing a general purpose GeoJSON feature extractor from any random collection of Features and GeometryCollections and the like is harder than just parsing the stereotyped data coming out of the ESRI source. I wouldn't try to do that without good examples and tests.

(BTW, the JSON in `cache.json` we write and serve right now is not valid JSON. The last Feature has a comma at the end, JSON doesn't allow that. Both Python's `json` and the JSON tool `jq` won't parse it.)

so here's the relevant code from `aiohttp.client.ClientSession.request`:

```python
            req = self._request_class(
                method, url, params=params, headers=headers,
                skip_auto_headers=skip_headers, data=data,
                cookies=cookies, encoding=encoding,
                auth=auth, version=version, compress=compress, chunked=chunked,
                expect100=expect100,
                loop=self._loop, response_class=self._response_class,
                proxy=proxy, proxy_auth=proxy_auth, timeout=timeout)

            with Timeout(timeout, loop=self._loop):
                conn = yield from self._connector.connect(req)
```

note that timeout is sent to the request_class, which is used for the stream read.  So as long as your read_timeout is > than your connect timeout (which should be default) it should be fine.

Yeah, probably we should disallow `+` symbols from being in assignment names going forward.

In your case as a work around, you should be able to rename the folders in the `submitted` directory which I think should fix the problem. Here is a little script that should do what you want:

```
import os
import shutil
for filename in os.listdir('submitted'):
    if '+' not in filename:
        continue
    student, _ = filename.split('+', 1)
    if not os.path.exists(os.path.join('submitted', student)):
        os.makedirs(os.path.join('submitted', student))
    for assignment in os.listdir(os.path.join('submitted', filename)):
        shutil.move(os.path.join('submitted', filename, assignment), os.path.join('submitted', student, assignment))
    os.removedirs(os.path.join('submitted', filename))
```

I am fine with "a visual treatment that is more pronounced and uses more
color" - but simply coloring *everything* without any attempt to create a
design that works well isn't a solution. I don't think it is a matter of
picking a different color - to do that right we would need to change the
DOM structure of a cell to carefully constraint which parts of highlighted.

But more importantly, our conceptual model is completely different from
both text editors and selection UIs such as Gmail. In both of those cases,
actions *never* apply to the cursor. In our case, they *always* apply to
the cursor. That super important fact isn't reflected in this design.

The final point that I feel is important is that orthogonal concepts should
be represented using orthogonal visual encodings in a minimal and
consistent manner:

* Solid blue backgrouns is being used to warn the user about which cells
the action will apply to - but that encoding is not on the cursor cell
where most actions will be applied to.
* The cursor cell is encoded visually using both a grey border and the left
blue thick border.
* If you have large cells with lots of content, even selected a few of them
will turn your entire screen blue (or another color). That is maximal
visual encoding, not minimal.
* The solid blue shading is so strong that it is really hard to see where
your cursor cell. Tracking it is very important as some actions (Return for
edit mode) will only apply to it.

@Carrea - as an aside, it is really difficult to evaluate visual design
decisions when animated. Animations are perfect for evaluating interactions
and behaviors. Could you post a static image of the visual design along
with the animation?

I just released a new Foolbox version (1.3.2). With that one, changing the shape in the preprocessing function is fully supported:

Standard PyTorch example from the docs:
```python3
import torchvision.models as models
import numpy as np
import foolbox

resnet18 = models.resnet18(pretrained=True).cuda().eval()  # for CPU, remove cuda()
mean = np.array([0.485, 0.456, 0.406]).reshape((3, 1, 1))
std = np.array([0.229, 0.224, 0.225]).reshape((3, 1, 1))
model = foolbox.models.PyTorchModel(
    resnet18, bounds=(0, 1), num_classes=1000,
    preprocessing=(mean, std), channel_axis=1)

image, label = foolbox.utils.imagenet_example(data_format='channels_first')
image = image / 255
print(np.argmax(model.predictions(image)), label)
print(foolbox.utils.softmax(model.predictions(image))[label])

attack = foolbox.attacks.GradientAttack(model)
adv = attack(image, label, unpack=False)
print(adv.distance)
```

Modified PyTorch example with a preprocessing function converting from (N)HWC to (N)CHW:
```python3
import torchvision.models as models
import numpy as np
import foolbox

resnet18 = models.resnet18(pretrained=True).cuda().eval()  # for CPU, remove cuda()

def preprocessing(x):
    mean = np.array([0.485, 0.456, 0.406]).astype(x.dtype)
    std = np.array([0.229, 0.224, 0.225]).astype(x.dtype)
    x = (x - mean) / std
    assert x.ndim in [3, 4]
    if x.ndim == 3:
        x = np.transpose(x, axes=(2, 0, 1))
    elif x.ndim == 4:
        x = np.transpose(x, axes=(0, 3, 1, 2))
    def grad(dmdp):
        assert dmdp.ndim == 3
        dmdx = np.transpose(dmdp, axes=(1, 2, 0))
        dmdx = dmdx / std
        return dmdx
    return x, grad

model = foolbox.models.PyTorchModel(
    resnet18, bounds=(0, 1), num_classes=1000,
    preprocessing=preprocessing, channel_axis=3)

image, label = foolbox.utils.imagenet_example(data_format='channels_last')
image = image / 255
print(np.argmax(model.predictions(image)), label)
print(foolbox.utils.softmax(model.predictions(image))[label])

attack = foolbox.attacks.GradientAttack(model)
adv = attack(image, label, unpack=False)
print(adv.distance)
```

All right, let's tackle this.

First I should mention that custom attributes are kind of meant to go in the `app_data` parameter, which will be made available during the loop as `context.app_data`. Using `context` is mostly fine, though — it's just that there is currently no namespacing for Schematics vs. private options.

The reason the code fails with an actual `Context` object is that the exact same object is being used first for model instantiation (import) and later export. This is a huge no-no, and it's really good this came up because we need to make sure this is well documented. `Context` instances encapsulate all kinds of options regarding the import/export process, and reusing them (even when it *does* work) will lead to unpredictable behavior unless you're aiming to replicate the settings on purpose.

However, passing a **freshly constructed** `Context` instance instead of a `dict` is perfectly fine and does work. It's even necessary in cases where the initiating code needs to retain a reference to the context after it's been populated in `import_loop` or `export_loop`.

All of that said, the PR itself is good. While we're at it, would you mind replicating it to test the `app_data` parameter as well?

We are definitely open to this sort of feature, but I want to make sure I understand more specifically before I can make any recommendations on how to implement it.  Is the desire to have the row count of affected rows before or after the Cursor is completely exhausted?  In the case of a select query, the ActivityCount may be accurate before reading through the results, although I think I remember trying to add that and it not working consistently as I expected.  This I believe is why we ultimately made the decision to just allow the user to count the rows for select statement returns themselves (not to say a nice property can't be added to do that for you, resetting on each execute for instance).  The information about what rows were deleted or updated may also be returned but I also seem to remember them being inconsistent as well.

I think regardless, this would be a good addition to the code, and fairly easy to implement.  It would involve adding a switch statement [here](https://github.com/capitalone/giraffez/blob/master/giraffez/src/teradata.c#L268) for `PclSUCCESS` and doing something similar to what it is doing with `PclFAILURE` [here](https://github.com/capitalone/giraffez/blob/master/giraffez/src/teradata.c#L291-L293) by creating a [function](https://github.com/capitalone/giraffez/blob/master/giraffez/src/teradata.c#L42) that reads from the data pointer and casts it into the appropriate struct provided by the CLIv2 (`PclSUCCESStype` in `parcel.h`).

So yeah, just let me know your thoughts on what the rowcount property should be (the Cursor keeps up with it if it can, it always returns the ActivityCount, some mix of the two, etc) and we can go from there!

In short, the [CLIv2/TPTAPI](https://capitalone.github.io/giraffez/FAQ.html#what-are-the-teradata-call-level-interface-version-2-and-teradata-parallel-transporter-api-and-how-do-i-get-them) are official Teradata C/C++ libraries for interacting with Teradata server.  There are some nuance but that is mostly the reason these were used.

The CLIv2, TPTAPI, JDBC, and ODBC are all various methods for interfacing with Teradata server.  They all differ greatly in capability and implementation.  The most straightforward is ODBC, which uses the common ODBC API for which Teradata implements to some degree.  The CLIv2, however, is the official low-level call library for which almost all client capabilities are derived (written in C).  For example, ODBC in some manner is most likely implemented (on the server-side) with some (or a lot of) interaction with the CLIv2.  Giraffez chose this as its library so that it could have the most powerful interface to Teradata that is provided (in performance and capability).  Teradata has implemented bulk loading procedures (fastload, multiload, etc) in C++, using the CLIv2 at its core and is where the TPTAPI comes in (Teradata Parallel Transporter API).  Giraffez uses this to perform bulk load procedures not available to ODBC or naively via CLIv2.

JDBC ends up being a mix (functionally) of both ODBC and Teradata has chosen to re-implement the bulk protocols purely in Java.  Ultimately what this means is that the JDBC is capable of doing "normal" SQL interactions with Teradata, while also being able to do bulk protocols like fastload/multiload or fastexport.

Giraffez uses the CLIv2 and the TPTAPI, under the hood, specifically to provide the greatest surface area for interacting with Teradata possible.  The advantages, for instance, over ODBC is that ODBC does not allow for usage of the bulk protocols.  The other advantages are being able to bind C/C++ with Python code vs. something like translating Java types to Python types with the JDBC.

The examples here make the common assumption that there is a single relation with a given name ('mysql'), whereas there might be several (mysql:1, mysql:2 etc.). This proposal will look very different with this addressed. If the framework is going to automatically map sets of units to the flag, it needs to use the relation id as a namespace. So maybe:

```python
@when('relation.mysql.database.available')
def update_database():
    for relid, mysql in flag('relation.mysql.database.available').items():
        render_config(db=mysql.dsn())
        break  # Just render an arbitrary one.
```

And for the common case where you aren't writing backend systems or proxies and really do only have a single relation with the given name:

```python
@when('relation.mysql.database.available')
def update_database():
    mysql = flag('relation.mysql.database.available').value  # or maybe 'one', 'any', 'first'
    rebder_config(db=mysql.dsn())
```

I think the above also addresses some confusion I had with the naming, where I think of flags as booleans, yet the flag() function was returning an object with state and behaviour. Attaching or annotating a flag with one or more relations is conceptually better to me than subclassing a Flag class to implement the object representing my relation.

When I did my branch making RelationBase pluggable, I was thinking that perhaps it was unfair that charms.reactive.bus.set_state was only supposed to accept a RelationBase as a value. Why not accept anything that can be serialized? By doing this, existing code using RelationBase would continue to work (or not), and new code could attach other types of relation object... in this case a Flag... or whatever else they found useful. For example, set_state('app.status', 'active') could attach a string and a @when('app.status') decorated method would get the status string as its argument automatically, instead of the current approach of having to stuff it into unitdata.kv() and retrieve it yourself. However, this proposal is doing away with parameters to handlers. Which is probably a good thing, but the change unlikely to be backwards compatible so should be explicitly called out.

#### How to override ShippingAddressForm and UserAddressForm without repeating yourself ####

```python
# apps.address.mixins
from django import forms

from localflavor.us import us_states as states
from localflavor.us import forms as us_forms

from oscar.core.loading import get_model

Country = get_model('address', 'Country')

class USAddressMixin(object):
    """ Mixin that customizes an AddressForm to make it more US-specific.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(USAddressMixin, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.fields['state'] = us_forms.USStateField(label="State", widget=forms.Select(choices=states.CONTIGUOUS_STATES),
                                                     help_text = "Only orders going to Kansas are liable for Sales tax")
        self.fields['line1'].label = "Address"
        self.fields['postcode'].label = "Zip code"

        if 'country' in self.fields:
            self.fields['country'].queryset = Country.objects.filter(pk='US')
            self.fields['country'].widget = forms.HiddenInput()
            self.fields['country'].initial = Country.objects.get(pk='US')

        for key in ('line2', 'line3'):
            if key in self.fields:
                del self.fields[key]
```

I propose that `letsencrypt-auto` should be called `letsencrypt-bootstrap`, which does the installation part, then a `letsencrypt` binary placed in `VENV_PATH` which is pushed into `/usr/bin` that does the neccesary actions to make the real `letsencrypt` work.

Lately, I've been thinking a lot about multiple webroots and today I've been thinking about how to best specify them on the command line. The interface proposed in #1594 is pretty good, but I'm wondering if that's the best approach. Sure, we could always change the interface later, but I'd rather take a few extra days to get the interface right the first time instead of causing a breaking change later.

Unfortunately, I haven't been able to come up with a CLI I really like. The main problem I'm having is mapping webroots to domains. The way I see it, there are a couple approaches:

#### Include mapping in webroot-path flag
This looks something like `--webroot-path /var/www/html:example.com`. My main problem here is duplication. If you're specifying everything on the command line, you must include `--domains` meaning that you have to specify each domain in both `--webroot-path` and in `--domains`. This is excessive.

We can optimize this approach, however. The best I have come up with is using a range in `--webroot-path`, similar to how you specify lines in `git blame`. For example, 
```
letsencrypt --webroot --domains example.com,www.example.com,example.org,www.example.org,example.net,www.example.net --webroot-path /var/www/html:example.com,www.example.org --webroot-path /usr/share/nginx/html:example.net,www.example.net
```

`/var/www/html` will be used for: `example.com,www.example.com,example.org,www.example.org`
`/usr/share/nginx/html` will be used for: `example.net,www.example.net`

The details would have to be worked out. Can the start or end of the range be optional? What happens if you don't provide a range? What if two ranges overlap?

#### CLI option order matters
This is the approach proposed in #1594. Basically, you propose some kind of ordering saying that the `--webroot-path` flag applies to all domains before/after the flag that don't already have a webroot.

My problems with this approach are we've done a fair amount of work to make sure that CLI order doesn't matter. Additionally, I think this behavior is unintuitive for those familiar with *nix command lines. This doesn't mean much, but can anyone point me to a successful CLI program where the ordering of the flags (not things like subcommands/options without flags) matters?

The benefit of this approach is it's less verbose. It's reducing verbosity by imposing restrictions on command line ordering.

#### Other
Unfortunately, I haven't come up with any other approaches. You either state the mapping in a single flag (which requires duplication) or impose restrictions on command line ordering.

If anyone has any thoughts on this, I'd love to hear them.

The "Installing Python packages..." step may take a few minutes. Unfortunately, `letsencrypt-auto` hides all `pip` output, even in when `--verbose` is provided on the command line (there can be quite a bit of output sometimes). I created #2835 to track that issue.

In the meantime, if you think you gave `letsencrypt-auto` enough time on your system, you can install our Python dependencies manually to see if you get the same behavior.

1. Run `export XDG_DATA_HOME=${XDG_DATA_HOME:-~/.local/share}` to create an environment variable for where to install the Python virtual environment.

2. Run `rm -rf $XDG_DATA_HOME/letsencrypt` to remove any partial installation that may exist.

3. Run `virtualenv --no-site-packages --python $(command -v python2.7 || command -v python27 || command -v python2 || command -v python) $XDG_DATA_HOME/letsencrypt` to create a new virtual environment.

4. Upgrade your virtual environment `curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/v0.5.0/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/pipstrap.py | PATH=$XDG_DATA_HOME/letsencrypt/bin:$PATH $XDG_DATA_HOME/letsencrypt/bin/python -`

5. Install `letsencrypt` and it's dependencies. `$XDG_DATA_HOME/letsencrypt/bin/pip install --no-cache-dir --require-hashes -r <(curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/v0.5.0/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/letsencrypt-auto-requirements.txt)`

If you're still with me, you will have just accomplished the same thing as `letsencrypt-auto`. If the script hung, please let us know where, otherwise, you can now use `letsencrypt-auto` which will recognize that `letsencrypt` has been installed.

I didn't intend to directly speed  up `letsencrypt-auto` --- but rather `letsencrypt` itself.  As you say, it's quite quick already, but it can be faster.

For me, the wall-times for the following non-interactive commands are:

* `letsencrypt --version`
 * without `demandimport`: 0.27
 * with `demandimport`:  0.11
* `letsencrypt certonly -d notfqdn`
 * without `demandimport`: 0.28
 * with `demandimport`: 0.12
* `letsencrypt --help`
 * without `demandimport`: 0.27
 * with `demandimport`:  0.11

For these commands that immediately return, the change is quite noticeable.

I also ran `letsencrypt` to request certificates and the wall-times were quite high and variable because of the communication with the server.  I list here the user-space CPU time:

* `letsencrypt certainly -d properdomain -a webroot --webroot-path /my/webroot`
 * without `demandimport`: 0.42
 * with `demandimport`: 0.28

So here, with the high wall-time of 4 seconds, you don't really notice the difference.

So the advantage lies with commands that exit immediately (without communicating with a server) (or go into a prompt immediately, although I did not measure this).

The disadvantage is that it may cause surprising bugs for someone who is not aware of `demandimport` running.  For instance, if a third-party module uses something like

```python
try:
    import somemodule as somepurpose
except ImportError:
    import someothermodule as somepurpose
```

Then even if `somemodule` does not exist, it will still assume it does as the `ImportError` isn't raised immediately.  Later on, on first use of `somepurpose` the `ImportError` will be raised.  The solution is to add `demandimport.ignore('some module')` after `demandimport.enable()`.

Consider this (over simplified) code:

```
import serial

def f():
    s=serial.Serial("/dev/ttyUSB1")                                                                                                      
    s.write(b"aaa")
```

Running this in a terminal while having another terminal open in read produces the expected result, the write operation is performed immediately.

So, the open() method actually returns when the port is ready.

Now, I'm no expert, but I think the issue that you are having has nothing to do with how the pyserial library reports port readiness, but rather with how serial buffers are managed along the transmission line.

A (rudimental) solution to your situation might be to just wait for the first byte and then attempt to read, like so:

```
import unittest
import serial
import time

"""
1) create a virtual or real connection between COM12 and COM13
2) in a terminal connected to COM12 (at 9600, N81), enter some junk text (e.g.'sdgfdsgasdg')
3) then execute this unit test
"""

class Test_test1(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_A(self):
        with serial.Serial(port='COM13', baudrate=9600) as s:   # open serial port
            while not s.in_waiting:
                time.sleep(0.01)
            print("Read ASAP:  {}".format(s.read(s.in_waiting)))
            time.sleep(0.1)     # wait for 100 ms for pyserial port to actually be ready
            print( "Read after delay:  {}".format(s.read(s.in_waiting)) )

if __name__ == '__main__':
    unittest.main()
```

In theory, checking if a BREAK condition is active is close to reading the state of the RX pin. But pySerial currently does not provide a function to check for a BREAK condition (not so easily done cross platform).

since your input dimension length is 2, according to https://github.com/apple/coremltools/blob/master/coremltools/converters/keras/_keras2_converter.py#L227, it assumes your input shape is `[Seq, D]`, and therefore returns `(dim[1],)` where dim would be `(126,12)` in your case.

To get around this, you could try:
```
inputlayer = Input(shape=(126 * 12,))

model = Reshape((126,12))(inputlayer)
model = BatchNormalization()(model)
model = Conv1D(16, 25, activation='relu')(model)
model = Flatten()(model)
model = Dense(output_size, activation='sigmoid')(model)

model = Model(inputs=inputlayer, outputs=model)
```
where in your coreML app, input a flattened input thats of shape `(126 * 12,)`, and the mlmodel will handle the reshaping to `(126, 12)` within the model.

    def test_no_input_sequence():
        num_batch, seq_len, n_features = 2, 3, 4
        x_in = np.random.random((num_batch, n_features)).astype('float32')
        l_inp = InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))

        lasagne.random.get_rng().seed(1234)
        cell_inp = InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))
        cell = CustomRecurrentCell(
            None, None, DenseLayer(cell_inp, n_features), hid_init=l_inp)['output']
        l_rec_1 = lasagne.layers.RecurrentContainerLayer(
            {}, cell, n_steps=seq_len, only_return_final=True)

        l_rec_2 = l_inp
        for i in range(seq_len):
            lasagne.random.get_rng().seed(1234)
            l_rec_2 = DenseLayer(l_rec_2, n_features)

        assert helper.get_output_shape(l_rec_1, x_in.shape) == (2, 4)

        output_1 = helper.get_output(l_rec_1).eval({l_inp.input_var: x_in})
        output_2 = helper.get_output(l_rec_2).eval({l_inp.input_var: x_in})
        assert np.allclose(output_1, output_2)

Thank you for the report, and for the fix (and for the debian package!). Unfortunately, it fails pep8 (needs two blank lines before a function definition) and reduces test coverage. Make sure this isn't a problem for the package.

Reading up on `getargspec`, a more compatible replacement is `getfullargspec`: https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/inspect.html#inspect.getfullargspec
Unfortunately, while this is allowed in Python 3.6, it was deprecated in Python 3.5, and so it will still trigger the deprecation warnings (that's a fun way to fail the tests, by the way -- they expect to get a single warning about the unused keyword in `get_output`, but then get two deprecation warnings extra). So we're stuck with `signature`.

If we're making this a separate function, I'd suggest to have it return the keyword arguments right away:
```python
def getkwargs(func):
   try:
       params = inspect.signature(func).parameters
       return [p.name for p in params.values() if p.default is not p.empty]
   except AttributeError:  # pragma: no cover
       spec = inspect.getargspec(func)
       return spec.args[-len(spec.defaults):] if spec.defaults else []
```
This would simplify the remaining code a bit, and make it easier to write a test for this helper function.

This is because we are using the (concretized) filename to name symbolic variables in the file content. Z3 (or Z3Py) requires all symbolic variables to have printable names. When the filename is concretized to something unprintable, you will see this crash.

The solution is to not use filenames to name symbolic variables.

When there are multiple hashes in the same bucket it will first compare the hash.
If the hashes are equal it will check `is`.
If `is` returns `False` it will check `==`, but only if `is` returned `False`

So it will only check `==` if the hashes are equal but `is` returns `False`

A while ago as part of the performance problems it was observed there was a severe overhead with large list like objects in Ganga 6.1.
In order to try and combat this I tried to use a ```queues``` based system for initializing many objects and constructing a huge list with them which used to be done many times throughout the assignment process.
Unfortunately testing later showed that this code offered no performance improvement and list construction no longer appears to be the bottleneck.

However, the code remained.
As Ulrik pointed out this creates a bug that the assembled final list is not an ordered list so instead of trying to add complex logic to fix the multi-threaded case (which offered no performance improvement after-all) I've opted to drop to the single threaded case and remove the multi-threaded code which would now be inaccessible or commented out.

This PR also addresses the fact that there was object manipulation going on in the Proxy setter which was incorrect and was at least slowing down the assignment process and at worst potentially corrupting objects which were being externally passed to the setter as the new value.

aka, in some situations the following could potentially fail:
```
myObject = GangaObjectClass()
myObject2 = GangaObjectClass()
myObject.someAttribute = myObject2.someAttribute
assert isProxy(myObject2.someAttribute) != False
```
The following could lead to failure as the input value to the assignment ```myObject2.someAttribute``` was accidentally redefined as the proxy-stripped version of itself within the setter.

I'm kind of tying myself into knots here. The problem I'm facing is that if you delete a bucket and then a collection from inside that bucket, the event listener for buckets deletes the quota record for the bucket, and then the quotas plugin emits a nonsensical quota record for the bucket (-1 records, -x size). If you do it the other way around, the quota record is correct, and then the event listener for the bucket deletes it. When I see this, I see a dependency on the bucket listener by the quotas plugin, and my view ends up depending on both of them.

Now, if the buckets event listener doesn't emit events for deleting its children, then you end up with something like the current quotas plugin. When a collection is deleted, it does a `get_all` to fetch the records that it "knows" will be deleted, and takes them into account, and it "knows" that it doesn't have to clean up its quota entries because they'll be deleted by the buckets/collections event listeners.

If the buckets plugin *does* emit events for deleting its children, then the quotas plugin gets both simpler and more complicated, because it no longer has to do a `get_all` to fetch records, but it has to accommodate the fact that when an event shows up for a deleted record, it may be a "cascade" from a deleted bucket that it already saw. I can't tell, but this might mean that there's still knowledge "baked in" to the quotas plugin that says what kind of objects are children of what other kinds of objects.

I don't love either of these approaches. I think if you care about `record` deletion, you shouldn't have to also listen to `collection` and `bucket` deletion. My feeling is that the most correct thing to do is to delete the children before deleting the parent, but that can't be made to work with the `buckets` event listener as it stands today (because it only happens after the bucket was deleted).
