Fundamentally, index-based cursors are never going to work, because you need to full context of the list to know what the index will be. In a relay context, that means a different cursor for every sort/filter combo that might be addressing the edge. The data required for that is out of control, IMO.

Stepping back, the only requirement for a cursor is that you can select before and after it. Thus, the simplest type of non-index cursor is the sort value for the list. For example, say you have a list of objects with `created_date` fields, ordered by `created_date`. In that case, your cursor could literally be the `created_date` value for the object, since you can always `.filter(created_date__gt=cursor)` to slice the results.

Optionally, if you want the sort field to be dynamic, you can use an object's unique `id`. In that case you would need to query the sort value matching that `id` and perform a similar query.

As far as I can tell, once of these two options is required to solve this issue.

Hi @mwilliamson-healx,
At first congrats for your great proof of concept!

I've been thinking for a while how we can improve performance in GraphQL. This repository -`graphene`- uses `graphql-core` under the hood which is a very similar port of the GraphQL-js reference implementation.

The problem we are seeing is that either in `graphql-core` and `graphql-js` that **each type/value is checked in runtime** (*what I mean is that the resolution+serialization function is "discovered" in runtime each time a value is completed*). In js the performance difference is minimal as usually have a great `JIT` that optimizes each of the type/value completion calls. However as Python doesn't have any `JIT` by default, this result in a quite expensive operation.

In the current `graphql-js` and `graphql-core` implementations if you want to execute a GraphQL query this is how the process will look like:

```
Parse AST from string (==> validate the AST in the given schema) ==> Execute a AST given a Root type.
```

However we can **create a "_Query Builder_"** as intermediate step before executing that will know exactly what are the fields we are requesting and therefore it's **associated types** and **resolvers**, so we don't need to "search" for them each time we are completing the value.
This way, the process will be something like:

```
Parse AST from string (==> validate the AST in the given schema) ==> Build the Query resolver based in the AST ==> Execute the Query resolver builder given a Root type.
```

Your [proof of concept](https://github.com/mwilliamson/python-graphjoiner) is doing the latter so the performance difference is considerable comparing with the current `graphql-core` implementation.

I think it's completely reasonable to introduce this extra `Query resolver` build step before executing for avoid the performance bottleneck of doing it in runtime. In fact, I would love to have it in `graphql-core`.

And I also think this would be a great idea to add in the `graphql-js` implementation as it will improve performance there too and push other language implementations ( @leebyron ).

I believe you need the output shape to use the underlying tensorflow tf.nn.conv2d_transpose function (https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/nn/conv2d_transpose). It might be possible to infer the shape. Inferring it can be difficult though in cases where stride’s > 1. For example, if you apply a plain conv2d operation on a 20x20 image with a stride of 2, the output image will be 10x10. Likewise, if you apply a plain conv2d operation on a 21x21 image with a stride of 2, the output will also be 10x10. Now suppose you apply a conv_2d_transpose function on an image size 10x10 with a stride of 2, will the output be size 20x20 or 21x21? The easy way to solve that ambiguity is to just pass in the desired output shape. There might be other tricks I’m unaware of. Share if you have any ideas.

```
import re

            line = f.readline()

I was hinting at the complexity above.  For example, numpy builds wheels for 4 varieties of Python 2.7 (32/64 bits, narrow/wide unicode), then the Pythons 3 (32/64 bits).  You then want say 3 macOS wheels (Python 2.7, 3.5, 3.6).  The macOS wheels are not too taxing, but they do slow down your CI because there is only one macOS build at a time, and they tend to be slow to spin up on Travis.  For the multiple Linux wheels, you have build the wheels in a manylinux docker container, then run auditwheel over them.   This is what multibuild automates.  Of course you could have all these wheel builds in your main CI, but it will be slow, and usually unnecessary, because the chances are pretty small on any one commit, that you'll hit a wheel problem when your standard install is OK.

There's probably a few pieces that would be needed.

First - a Pulumi resource provider for managing Cloudflare resources.  That's something we're definitely interested in bringing up.  It could be done either on top of https://github.com/terraform-providers/terraform-provider-cloudflare using https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-terraform, or as a native Pulumi resource provider (similar to https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-kubernetes).   This is something we'd love to help with if anyone is interested in partnering on building it up.

That would give you the ability to manage raw resources (including Workers) in Cloudflare.  Next, you would want to create a library to allow you to use a higher-level programming model for connecting Cloudflare workers to API endpoints.  For that, you could take inspiration from https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws-serverless and create a `cloudflare-serverless` package.

These are on our longer term roadmap, but we're also open to helping out if others are interested in making progress on either/both in the near term.

the quick fix is simple: if perfect and immediate load balancing is desired (essentially gang scheduling), this can be done by forcing the local scheduler to forward all submitted tasks to the global scheduler. That way the global scheduler's resource accounting w.r.t. available capacity is always up to date. A mildly relaxed version of this fix that's worth giving serious consideration is to forward all GPU tasks (and actor placement requests) to the global scheduler by default. This should fix the load balancing issue reported here.

IMO one of the most important parts is the screenshots. They have the potential to convey a lot of information in a very short amount of time. The Livestreamer wiki suffers from a lack of screenshots, and makes choosing an interface a grueling affair, rather than the entertaining one it could be.

CLI (only) and GUI (which can be a mixture of both) should be separate sections. It would be frustrating as a user to sort through each one individually, like one would have to do for the Livestreamer wiki. Consider a site like Amazon. It would be exhausting to search for motherboards and keep finding dresses and fingernail clippers in the results.

Amongst the information that should be displayed, such as the name, url, screenshot and some kind of description, I think there should also be a section for what language it's written in, last time updated, and system requirements (such as supported OS's and any dependencies).

I also think the description should be one written by the community. When you develop something, you tend to see it as a developer, from the inside looking out. But the average person is seeing it from the outside looking in. They see only what their perception allows them to see. So an acuate description intended for the average user should be written by the average user.

I think the best approach to meet these needs is a table who's columns are sortable. So one could sort them by name, supported OS's (a separate column for each OS), last time updated, and even GUI or CLI. Would it be possible to generate this automatically?

IMOH ocrd workspace should always work with the current directory holding mets.xml as METS file.
WORKSPACE
Define the workspace.
Options:
-d --workspace-dir Path to workspace (optional) (default .)
-m --mets-file Name of the METS file inside workspaces root directory. (otpional) (default mets.xml)
Examples:
a) ocrd workspace -> cwd containing mets.xml
b) ocrd workspace -d /path/to/workspace -> /path/to/workspace containing mets.xml
c) ocrd workspace -m anotherMets.xml -> cwd containing anotherMets.xml
d) ocrd workspace -d /path/to/workspace -m anotherMets.xml -> /path/to/workspace containing anotherMets.xml
These two parameters define the whole workspace.
The following commands define the actions.
CLONE
Clone workspace to another directory. (name of METS file should be untouched)
All local files (file://...) will be copied to the new directory and METS file will reference to the
new location.
Options: 
-t --target-dir Target directory (will be created) existing directories are not allowed to avoid inconsistencies. (optional, if not defined a tempory directory will be created)
-f --force Overwrites/Remove existing data (optional)
-a --download-all Download all files and change location in METS file (optional)

FIND
Find file(s) inside METS file filtered by (USE, groupID, ID, mimetype). Output is defined by parameter -k
Options: 
  -G, --file-grp TEXT             fileGrp USE
  -m, --mimetype TEXT             Media type to look for
  -g, --group-id TEXT             GROUPID
  -k, --output-field [url|mimetype|groupId|ID|basename|basename_without_extension|local_filename]
                                  Output field
  -a, --download-all              Whether to download found files (if not already local)

PACK
  Pack workspace as ZIP
Options:
-z --zip-file Target file (should not exist) (required)
-f --force Overwrite target file (optional)
-a --download-all (Download and zip also remote files)

UNPACK
  Unpack ZIP as workspace
Options:
-z --zip-file Source file (should not exist) (required)
-t --target-dir Target directory (should not exist) (required)
-f --force Overwrite target directory (optional)

CREATE
  Create a workspace with an empty METS file.
Options:
  -m, --mets-file Name of the METS file. (default: mets.xml)
  -f, --force  Overwrite METS file if it exists

VALIDATE
  Validates workspace
Options:
No further options required.

LIST-GROUP
List the USE attribute of all defined fileGrps.
Options:
No further options required.

ADD
  Add a file to METS in a workspace.
Options:
  -G, --file-grp TEXT  fileGrp USE  [required]
  -i, --file-id TEXT   ID for the file  [required]
  -m, --mimetype TEXT  Media type of the file  [required]
  -u, --url TEXT       url
  -g, --group-id TEXT  GROUPID
  --help               Show this message and exit.

Thanks for reporting @itaimarts . I was able to reproduce this bug. 

Note that S3 metadata values are transmitted via HTTP header values. In your example, the metadata is transmitted using the following header:
```
x-amz-meta-__key1: val1
```

The root cause for this issue is that underscores in HTTP header names have traditionally been an issue (you can find more details if you google for "underscore header spoofing in CGI"). Therefore, most web frameworks are stripping underscores from HTTP header names, or ignore these headers entirely.

In our case, LocalStack makes use of [moto](https://github.com/spulec/moto), which uses the [flask](https://github.com/pallets/flask) framework to start local API servers. Flask is based on [werkzeug](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug) which provides an implementation of [WSGI](https://wsgi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) (the Python equivalent of CGI). Werkzeug is the culprit in our case. As you can see in [line 139 of serving.py](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/werkzeug/serving.py#L139), werkzeug creates an environment map by uppercasing all header names to variable names and replacing dashes with underscores:
```
        for key, value in self.headers.items():
            key = key.upper().replace('-', '_')
```

.. and then later when the environment variables are converted back to headers, underscores are converted back to dashes in [datastructurs.py](
https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/werkzeug/datastructures.py#L1360-L1364):
```
        for key, value in iteritems(self.environ):
            ...
                yield (key[5:].replace('_', '-').title(), _unicodify_header_value(value))
```
Given the tricky nature of this bug, I'm going to mark this as "Won't fix". Please feel free to bring up this issue in the upstream repositories (flask/werkzeug) to see if they can come up with a viable solution to pass HTTP headers with underscores to Flask apps. Note that there has been a similar bug request in the past which might be related: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/issues/940

At this point, I don't see an easy way we can fix this in LocalStack. I think the best workaround would be to not use underscores at all. If you find out more about this issue, please report back here. Thanks

storage systems should behave, if it was the Django developers would have
